inspector_license: AI-ASSISTED REVIEW — Requires licensed inspector sign-off
executive_summary: Overall, the property is in poor condition and exhibits several critical structural and system deficiencies that will severely impact its safety, structural integrity, and immediate insurability in the State of Florida. Most notably, significant foundation settlement and cracking at the southwest corner require immediate evaluation by a licensed structural or geotechnical engineer. Additionally, the home features multiple high-risk components at or near the end of their useful lives, including a failing 19-year-old shingle roof, active leaks in obsolete polybutylene water lines, and an unsafe Federal Pacific Electric Stab-Lok electrical panel with double-tapped breakers. Other notable defects requiring prompt professional correction include disconnected and uninsulated attic ductwork, an aged water heater with an improperly configured safety valve, and missing kitchen GFCI protection.

sections.headline: The 19-year-old asphalt shingle roof shows advanced wear, exposed fiberglass, and evidence of historic leakage, indicating it is nearing the end of its useful life.
sections.narrative:
During the inspection of the main roof's rear slope, we observed an asphalt shingle roof covering estimated to be approximately 19 years of age. The shingles exhibit signs of advanced deterioration, characterized by significant granule loss. Notably, the protective mineral granule coating has worn away, leaving the underlying fiberglass mat exposed in two distinct locations near the ridge vent. Additionally, there is evidence of a prior patch repair performed with mismatched shingles.

An inspection of the attic space directly beneath this area revealed historic water staining on the wood roof decking. While no active moisture or leaks were detected with a moisture meter at the time of the inspection, the staining is consistent with past water intrusion events. The combination of advanced age, localized substrate exposure, and historical leakage indicates the roof covering has reached the end of its reliable service life.

In accordance with Florida Building Code (FBC) Section R905 and standard insurance underwriting guidelines (such as the Citizens 4-Point Inspection Form HO-800), these findings constitute major roof deficiencies. Specifically, significant granule loss exposing the fiberglass membrane, mismatched repair materials, and evidence of past leaks on the decking are critical factors that typically impact a property's insurability and wind-mitigation ratings in the State of Florida.
action_items: Engage a licensed roofing contractor to perform a comprehensive evaluation of the entire roof surface and determine options for repair or replacement.
Obtain documented proof and permits for any previous or future roof repairs to ensure compliance with the Florida Building Code.
Monitor the stained areas of the attic decking during and after heavy rainfall events for any signs of active moisture intrusion until repairs are completed.
sections.inspector_note: This visual inspection is limited to readily accessible areas. Underlayment condition, flashing details beneath shingles, and the full structural integrity of the decking cannot be fully evaluated without removing the roof covering. This report is an assessment of conditions observed on the day of inspection and does not constitute a warranty or guarantee against future leaks.

sections.headline: A Federal Pacific Electric Stab-Lok main service panel was identified in the garage with active double-tapped breakers, posing a critical safety and insurance eligibility issue.
sections.narrative:
During the inspection of the electrical system, a 150-amp Federal Pacific Electric (FPE) Stab-Lok main distribution panel containing 32 circuit breakers was located in the garage. Although there was no visible evidence of active heat damage, arcing, or corrosion inside the enclosure at the time of the inspection, three double-tapped breakers were identified at circuits 4, 12, and 22. A double-tap condition occurs when two electrical conductors are secured under a single terminal screw that is not specifically designed or rated to accommodate multiple wires.

FPE Stab-Lok panels are widely recognized to have design and manufacturing defects associated with a high rate of breaker failures, including a failure to trip during overcurrent or short-circuit conditions. This can result in severe overheating of the branch circuit wiring. Furthermore, double-tapped connections can loosen over time, causing localized arcing, voltage drops, and increased fire risk.

In accordance with the National Electrical Code (NEC 2020), adopted in Florida via the Florida Building Code under Florida Statute 553.73, electrical equipment must be installed and maintained in strict accordance with its listing and labeling. FPE Stab-Lok panels are classified as high-risk electrical hazards by most property insurance underwriters in Florida. Consequently, this panel configuration is typically flagged as unacceptable on standard Citizens 4-Point Inspection Forms (HO-800), which may prevent the property from securing standard homeowners insurance coverage.
action_items: Retain a licensed electrical contractor to remove and replace the Federal Pacific Electric Stab-Lok electrical panel with a modern, UL-listed panel.

Ensure that the double-tapped conductors at circuits 4, 12, and 22 are corrected by the licensed electrical contractor to comply with National Electrical Code (NEC) single-conductor terminal standards.
sections.inspector_note: This inspection was visual only and did not include load testing, thermal imaging, or forecasting the future performance of the electrical equipment. Our findings are limited to the visible conditions present at the time of the inspection, in accordance with Florida Statute 468.8326.

sections.headline: Two kitchen countertop outlets located near the sink lack required Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI) safety protection.
sections.narrative:
During the inspection of the kitchen, two standard duplex electrical receptacles located within 36 inches of the sink were found to lack Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI) protection. No active GFCI receptacles or GFCI circuit breakers protecting these specific outlets were identified on the circuit. This condition represents an immediate safety hazard due to the close proximity of water and electrical current.

According to the National Electrical Code (NEC) Section 210.8, as adopted into the Florida Building Code via Florida Statute 553.73, GFCI protection is required for all electrical receptacles serving kitchen countertops and any outlets located within 6 feet of a water source. GFCI devices are designed to detect ground faults and shut off power instantly to prevent severe or fatal electrical shock.

In accordance with Florida home inspection reporting standards (Florida Statute 468.8326), this is classified as a physical deficiency requiring correction. While this represents a safety hazard rather than an immediate property insurance disqualification, correcting this issue is highly recommended to ensure occupant safety and to align with standard residential safety requirements.
action_items: Have a licensed Florida electrical contractor evaluate the kitchen countertop circuits.
Install GFCI-protected receptacles or a compatible GFCI circuit breaker to protect all outlets within 6 feet of the kitchen sink.
sections.inspector_note: This inspection is non-invasive and limited to visible and readily accessible areas. Hidden wiring behind walls, cabinets, or inside junction boxes was not inspected.

sections.headline: Active leak detected in gray polybutylene (Quest brand) supply piping, a material with a high failure history and significant insurance restrictions in Florida.
sections.narrative:
During the inspection of the plumbing system, gray polybutylene water supply piping (Quest brand, verified by stamped markings) was observed under the kitchen sink and visible within the crawl space access. This piping serves as the main hot and cold water distribution system throughout the home and is estimated to date from the original 1987 construction. Notably, an active pinhole leak with a steady drip was observed at a compression fitting under the kitchen sink at the time of inspection.

Polybutylene piping is known to degrade internally over time, leading to brittleness, micro-fracturing, and premature failure. Due to this high rate of failure, the presence of polybutylene supply lines is classified as a critical deficiency under Florida insurance standards, including the Citizens 4-Point Inspection guidelines (Form HO-800). Consequently, most Florida property insurance carriers consider this material an unacceptable risk and may deny coverage until the system is replaced.

Under Florida Statute 468.8326, inspectors must identify material deficiencies that require immediate repair or pose a risk of system failure. The combination of the active leak and the presence of aged polybutylene piping represents an immediate risk of water damage to the property. Complete system replacement is recommended to meet modern Florida Building Code (Chapter 6) standards and to satisfy typical property insurance underwriting requirements.
action_items: Engage a licensed plumber immediately to repair the active leak under the kitchen sink to prevent further moisture damage.

Obtain a comprehensive evaluation and estimate from a licensed plumbing contractor for a complete repipe of the home's water distribution system using approved materials (such as PEX, CPVC, or copper).
Consult with your insurance agent regarding the presence of polybutylene piping to understand its impact on policy eligibility and coverage in the state of Florida.
sections.inspector_note: This inspection is visual in nature and limited to readily accessible areas. Piping concealed behind wall coverings, under slab foundations, or beneath insulation could not be evaluated. A licensed plumbing contractor should perform a comprehensive assessment of the entire distribution system prior to closing.

sections.headline: A 15-year-old electric water heater has reached the end of its expected service life and features an improperly configured, hazardous TPR discharge pipe.
sections.narrative:
During the inspection of the garage, a 50-gallon electric water heater manufactured in 2009 (15 years old) was evaluated. While no active water leaks were observed at the tank or plumbing connections at the time of the inspection, the unit has reached the end of its typical 12-to-15-year useful service life.

A safety hazard was identified regarding the Temperature-Pressure Relief (TPR) valve. The discharge pipe currently terminates 8 inches above the floor and faces upward. In accordance with the Florida Building Code (FBC) Plumbing Chapter 6, TPR discharge piping must drain downward by gravity and terminate pointing downward within 6 inches of the floor (or to an approved location) to prevent scalding injuries if the valve activates.

Because the water heater is 15 years old and has an improperly discharged TPR valve, this component may be flagged as a deficiency on standard Florida insurance inspection forms (such as the Citizens 4-Point Form HO-800), which can affect the property's insurability. Correction by a licensed professional is recommended.
action_items: Consult with a licensed plumbing contractor to correct the TPR valve discharge pipe so that it drains downward and terminates safely in accordance with Florida Building Code requirements.
Budget for the replacement of the 15-year-old water heater, as it has reached the end of its expected useful service life.
sections.inspector_note: This inspection is visual only. The inner tank lining, heating elements, and future performance of the water heater cannot be verified. This summary does not constitute a warranty or guarantee of remaining service life.

sections.headline: The HVAC system is operational but exhibits major deficiencies, including disconnected and uninsulated attic ductwork.
sections.narrative:
At the time of inspection, we evaluated a 3-ton York split-system heat pump manufactured in 2011 (approximately 13 years old). The system was operational, demonstrating a temperature differential (delta-T) of 17°F across the evaporator coil, which is within the standard acceptable range of 15°F to 22°F. However, the system's air filter located in the utility closet was heavily soiled, suggesting deferred routine maintenance.

A physical inspection of the attic space revealed significant ductwork deficiencies. Two flexible duct joints have completely disconnected at the Y-branch junction serving the master bedroom and the den, causing conditioned air to discharge directly into the attic. Furthermore, the flexible ductwork within the attic space is entirely uninsulated. 

These conditions do not align with standard energy and mechanical guidelines. Under the Florida Building Code (FBC) Energy Conservation provisions, ductwork installed in unconditioned spaces such as attics must be insulated to a minimum of R-6 to prevent thermal loss and condensation. The disconnected duct runs also impact overall ventilation and indoor air quality as referenced in ASHRAE 62.2, and represent a deficiency trigger under common Florida insurance guidelines, such as the Citizens 4-Point Inspection (Form HO-800). Additionally, at 13 years of age, the equipment is approaching the end of its expected operational lifespan.
action_items: Retain a licensed HVAC contractor to properly reconnect, seal, and support the detached flexible duct joints at the Y-branch leading to the master bedroom and den.
Insulate all attic ductwork to meet the minimum R-6 thermal insulation standards to prevent energy loss and condensation.
Replace the heavily soiled air filter immediately and establish a regular maintenance schedule.
Have a licensed HVAC professional perform a comprehensive system clean, service, and safety evaluation.
sections.inspector_note: This evaluation is visual in nature. The inspector does not perform pressure testing, duct leakage testing, or measure refrigerant charge. A licensed HVAC technician should be consulted for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation.

sections.headline: Significant structural cracking and floor slope observed at the southwest corner, requiring immediate professional evaluation.
sections.narrative:
During the inspection of the structural systems, significant indicators of structural movement were observed at the southwest corner of the home, affecting both the living room and the adjacent bedroom wall. Specifically, a stair-step diagonal crack is present on the exterior concrete block wall, extending from the floor level up to approximately four feet in height. Corresponding cracking was also noted in the interior drywall at the same corner. Additionally, the floor in this area has a noticeable slope estimated at approximately 1 inch over an 8-foot span.

These physical findings are consistent with differential foundation settlement. Given that the property is located in Hillsborough County, which is characterized by karst geology, these conditions also align with recognized sinkhole indicators. In accordance with Florida home inspection standards and practices, home inspectors are not licensed to determine the presence of active sinkholes or diagnose underlying soil conditions.

Consequently, further specialized evaluation is required to determine the root cause of this structural movement and to establish necessary stabilization or repair protocols. A licensed professional engineer must be consulted to perform diagnostic testing and determine the precise cause of the settlement and the extent of repairs needed.
action_items: Engage a licensed structural engineer or geotechnical engineer to conduct a comprehensive structural and geological evaluation of the foundation and soil conditions at the southwest corner.
Consult a licensed general or structural contractor to obtain repair recommendations and cost estimates following the engineer's formal evaluation.
sections.inspector_note: This inspection represents a visual assessment of the home's visible structural components at the time of the inspection. The inspector is not a structural or geotechnical engineer and does not perform geological or sinkhole testing. Further specialized engineering evaluation is required to determine the safety and stability of the foundation.

limitations: This inspection is a visual, non-invasive examination of accessible areas only.
Areas not accessible (locked rooms, covered components) were not inspected.
Wood-destroying organism (WDO) inspection requires a separate licensed pest control operator.
Sinkhole determination requires a licensed geotechnical engineer.
Mold testing, if warranted, requires a separate licensed mold assessor.
This report does not constitute a warranty or guarantee of any system or component.
AI-generated narratives must be reviewed and approved by the licensed inspector of record.
footer: This report was prepared in accordance with Florida Statute 468 Part XV and the Florida Standards of Practice. For questions contact the inspector of record. FloridaInspect Agent — AI-assisted reporting system by MCAG Technologies.
